statement.p: Town A and Town B are 400 miles apart. With a constant still-water speed of 45 miles per hour, a boat traveled from Town A to Town B, and then back to Town A. During this whole trip, the river flew from Town B to Town A at a constant speed. The whole trip took 20 hours. Answer the following question:
During this trip, the rivers speed was miles per hour.

solution.p: Assume the rivers speed was x miles per hour. Its give that the boats still-water speed was 45 miles per hour.

Since the river flows from Town B to Town A, the boat traveled from Town A to Town B (against the current) at 45-x miles per hour, and then traveled back from Town B to Town A (along the current) at 45+x miles per hour.
Using the formula time=\frac{distance}{speed}, we know the boat traveled from Town A to Town B in \frac{400}{45-x} hours; and it traveled back from Town B to Town A in \frac{400}{45+x} hours.
Its given that the whole trip took 20 hours, so we can write an equation and solve it:
In this situation, x=-15 is not a valid solution, since speed cannot be negative. x=15 is the only solution.
During this trip, the rivers speed was {15} miles per hour.
